metadata.title: Evidence of Magma Reservoirs Beneath Volcanoes in Northern Sulawesi and the Molucca Sea From Regional Earthquake Tomography
metadata.study_area: Sulawesi island and surroundings — North Sulawesi subduction zone, Molucca Sea Collision Zone, Gorontalo Bay
metadata.method: Regional earthquake P-wave body wave tomography (FMTOMO); BMKG catalog 2018-2023; 126 permanent broadband stations; 6,207 earthquakes with 106,722 P-picks
conclusions: High velocity anomaly beneath Molucca Sea diverging E and W to ~200 km depth — divergent double subduction of Molucca Sea Plate (MSP): westward under Sangihe arc and eastward under Halmahera arc, dip ~45° each side
Low velocity anomalies at 80-130 km depth directly above Sangihe and Halmahera slabs at eastern end of North Arm — likely magma reservoirs (partial melt) supplying active arc volcanism

Colo volcano (Una-Una, Gorontalo Bay): diffuse low velocity zone at 60-110 km depth, distinct from sharper LVZs above slabs; character more consistent with decompression melting from extension (Hall & Spakman 2015 hypothesis) than subduction-driven melting
Celebes slab imaged to ~60 km depth in Vp model; resolution limited below this depth due to absence of deep earthquakes — consistent with slab only reaching ~200 km (Slab2 model)
Slow slip on subduction interface + deep afterslip on PKF (Nijholt et al. 2024) confirms subduction-transform edge propagator interaction at western trench terminus

properties.name: Molucca Sea Plate — Divergent Double Subduction High-Velocity Anomaly
properties.slab_system: Molucca Sea Plate (MSP) divergent double subduction
properties.interpretation: Continuous high-velocity anomaly in sections A-B and C-D diverging E and W to ~200 km depth, consistent with divergent double subduction of MSP (Zhang et al. 2017). West-dipping portion = Sangihe slab; east-dipping portion = Halmahera slab. Deep earthquake hypocenters confirm. Consistent with Hall & Spakman (2015) slab geometry.

properties.name: Sangihe/Halmahera Arc Magma Reservoirs — Low-Velocity Zones
properties.interpretation: Low-velocity anomalies directly above west- and east-dipping Molucca Sea slabs at eastern end of North Sulawesi Arm, at depths of ~80-130 km. Location corresponds to active Quaternary volcanoes in North Sulawesi and Molucca region. Interpreted as partial melt and/or fluid-saturated material forming magma reservoirs feeding arc volcanism. Amplitude suppressed by regularization — undamped models suggest up to 5% anomaly (consistent with partial melt).
properties.tectonic_context: Fluid release from Sangihe and Halmahera slabs at 80-130 km depth triggers melting in overlying mantle wedge; melt ascends to feed volcanic arc at eastern end of North Arm. No volcanism present west of ~124°E along North Arm despite south-dipping Celebes slab — absence unexplained by subduction-induced melting alone.

properties.name: Colo Volcano (Una-Una, Gorontalo Bay) — Diffuse Low-Velocity Zone
properties.location_description: Solitary island in Gorontalo Bay, ~100 km south of the North Arm coast
properties.interpretation: Diffuse low-velocity anomaly at 60-110 km depth beneath Colo, distinct from the more pronounced LVZs above Sangihe/Halmahera slabs. Character is more consistent with decompression melting from extension (crustal thinning, low-angle normal faulting, subduction rollback-driven subsidence in Gorontalo Bay) than subduction-induced melting. Hall & Spakman (2015) interpret Una-Una as product of delamination of Sula Spur as Sula slab becomes vertical. Also present in S-wave tomography model.
competing_hypotheses: Decompression melting from extension/crustal thinning (Hall & Spakman 2015, Pholbud et al. 2012) — FAVORED by this study
Subduction of Celebes slab beneath North Arm at shallow angle (Sendjaja 2013)
East Sangihe Subduction Zone volcanism extending SW (Effendi & Bawono 1997)
Adakites from slab melting of south-dipping Celebes slab (Vroon & van Bergen 2000)

properties.name: Celebes Slab — Shallow High-Velocity Imaged to ~60 km
properties.interpretation: High-velocity anomaly imaged to ~60 km depth in section E-F. Resolution drops off below this depth due to absence of deep earthquakes beneath Gorontalo Bay area. Consistent with Slab2 model showing south-dipping slab to ~200 km. The complete absence of Neogene or active arc volcanism above this slab (except Colo) is a key unexplained feature — no melting induced above the Celebes slab over most of its extent.
properties.no_arc_volcanism_note: No arc volcanism along most of the North Arm above the Celebes slab (unusual); only arc volcanism at far eastern end above Sangihe/Halmahera slabs; Colo (back-arc) explained by extension rather than slab melting
